chapterbody: ne year, winter came early to Seattle, Washington, USA. Early one morning, I looked out the window and saw that the fountain in the courtyard was frozen. Icicles hung down the sides of the fountain. It was beautiful like a crystal chandelier.

chapterbody 5: Cold
chapterbody 7: Wet
chapterbody 9: The temperature was below zero.
chapterbody 10: The house was surrounded by darkness
chapterbody 11: Wet was the winter rain of Seattle.
chapterbody 13: It was during this winter that I sat inside my study and wrote under the lamp this book, [Chatting with Oneself].

chapterbody: rom birth to old age to death, many people live their lives following a certain pattern. Let me simply list the sequence as follows:
chapterbody 2: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 3: Debuting at birth
chapterbody 5: Falling in love
chapterbody 7: Joining in and dissolution of marriage
chapterbody 9: Physical deterioration
chapterbody 10: Succumbing to disease
chapterbody 11: Portrait hanging on the wall
chapterbody 13: Oh heaven, this is life. There is a small fraction of people who live outside this program, but they are the exceptions to the rule. The majority of people live in accordance with this pattern.
chapterbody 15: So, the question is:
chapterbody 17: “Besides this, what else are you living for?”
chapterbody 19: I asked myself, what am I living for after all? There are some slight differences between what I do and what others do:
chapterbody 21: 1. Spiritual practice
chapterbody 22: 2. Writing
chapterbody 23: 3. Painting
chapterbody 26: I may claim the things I do are different from others, but in reality they are not any different. I simply play a special role, that of a “monk.”
chapterbody 28: We are all living inside this frame, similarly blind, bewildered, twisted, distorted, frustrated, experiencing good or bad times, facing slander…
chapterbody 30: All one’s life, one struggles.
chapterbody 31: One day, I finally jumped outside this frame!
chapterbody 32: Oh heaven! I was enlightened!
chapterbody 34: Question: “What are you doing with your life after all?”
chapterbody 35: My reply: “Now I have finally understood. I have not done anything at all!” (Key words.)
chapterbody 38: My reply: “Let me give an example. Now that I have written altogether 246 books, I am proclaiming that I have basically not written a single word.”
chapterbody 40: Question: “People of the world will not understand what you are saying!”
chapterbody 41: My reply: “Shakyamuni Tathagata also said that after teaching the dharma for forty-nine years, he had not spoken a single word.”
chapterbody 43: Question: “People of the world do not understand why the Buddha said this!”
chapterbody 44: My reply: “I do. The Buddha indeed did not give any discourse, and I indeed did not write anything.”
chapterbody 46: Question: “Why is it that you understand the Buddha while the general populace does not?”
chapterbody 47: My reply: “I am Shakyamuni Tathagata.”
chapterbody 49: Question: “Are you then the reincarnation of Shakyamuni Buddha?”
chapterbody 50: My reply: “We are equivalent. What Shakyamuni Buddha has intuited and realized is exactly what I have intuited and realized.”
chapterbody 52: My reply: “I do not care what others say, believe or disbelieve, suspect or do not suspect, I have always been a buddha.”
chapterbody 53: Question: “You say such bold words shamelessly!”
chapterbody 55: Question: “Such arrogance. Are you a megalomaniac?”
chapterbody 56: My reply: “Those are bold words, and I am not ashamed to say them!”

chapterbody 17: I will tell you a small secret.
chapterbody 19: In the first story, the key is the sound “smack”!
chapterbody 20: In the past there was no “smack”!
chapterbody 21: The “smack” lasted only a few seconds and was gone.
chapterbody 22: In the future, there will be no “smack”!
chapterbody 23: It is neither in the past, present, nor future. Hence he became enlightened. This “smack” sound appeared out of thin air when it had previously been non-existent. Existence is also non-existence. Isn’t this enlightenment then?

chapterbody 26: Before the manufacturing of the cup, there was no cup.
chapterbody 27: After dropping to the floor and breaking into pieces, the cup is no more!
chapterbody 28: In the future, that cup will not exist!
chapterbody 30: Upon realization that the cup did not exist in the past, does not exist in the present, and will not exist in the future, he attained enlightenment!

chapterbody 61: Sometimes, it felt like we were rushing and in a hurry. But, our affinities stretch throughout hundreds, thousands, and millions of lifetimes…
chapterbody 63: Question: “Why are the encounters so short?”
chapterbody 65: My reply: “Human life is also like the lightning’s flash!”
chapterbody 67: Time is ruptured.
chapterbody 68: Indeed, under the vast and spacious sky, human generations move on and change.
chapterbody 70: The Karmapa said: “Generally, I practice the Tara Sadhana in the morning and the protector sadhana in the evening.”

chapterbody: t the first dharma ceremony after my return to Taiwan on November 22, 2014, a student captured two light photographs.
chapterbody 3: In one photograph, I am holding a vajra bell facing upward. From the opening of the bell, a beautiful beam of red light radiates. In the other photograph, I am holding a mudra with the right index finger in a vertical position. From the tip of the index finger a white circular light with a touch of red and gold is emitted. This light even has its own halo, which is quite remarkable.
chapterbody 5: The photographs were passed around, and many people found them rare and inconceivable.
chapterbody 7: Someone asked: “Are these real?”
chapterbody 9: My reply: “That is my hand. It is of course real.”
chapterbody 11: That person asked: “What I meant was, are the lights real?”
chapterbody 13: My reply: “My hands are of course real. As for the lights, please ask the lights, okay?”
chapterbody 15: That person was speechless.
chapterbody 17: Let me tell everyone:
chapterbody 19: This is the same hand I hold chopsticks with when I eat.
chapterbody 20: This is the same hand I hold brushes with when I paint.
chapterbody 21: This is the same hand I hold toilet paper with when I clean my butt.
chapterbody 22: This is the same hand I stretch out to give everyone mo-ding.
chapterbody 23: When this hand of mine emits light, it is still my hand!
chapterbody 25: I am someone who has attained mingxin jianxing (understanding the nature of mind and recognizing the Buddha-essence), and it is not strange at all that my palms emit light.
chapterbody 27: Around my head is a halo.
chapterbody 28: Behind my back, a light shines.
chapterbody 30: Lotus lights are under my feet.
chapterbody 31: Light emanates from every pore of my body.
chapterbody 32: When I consecrate a Buddhist statue, the statue will emit light.
chapterbody 33: When I hold a vajra bell, the vajra bell will definitely emit light.
chapterbody 35: All tantrikas who attain great achievements in the Great Perfection
chapterbody 36: Dharma will ultimately transform into rainbow light and ascend into
chapterbody 38: In my earlier books, I often wrote about the three lights on my
chapterbody 39: body:
chapterbody 40: Buddha light.
chapterbody 41: Spiritual light.
chapterbody 43: I have had the following conversation with myself –
chapterbody 45: “When you rang the vajra bell, did you know the vajra bell radiated light?”
chapterbody 47: My reply: “I did not know.”
chapterbody 49: “When you extended your index finger, did you know the index finger was emitting light?”
chapterbody 51: My reply: “I did not know.”
chapterbody 52: “When the student took those pictures, did you know?”
chapterbody 53: My reply: “I did not know.”
chapterbody 54: “If you did not know any of those, what do you know?”
chapterbody 58: My reply: “I know of not knowing.”
chapterbody 60: “What is the esoteric meaning of this?”
chapterbody 63: My reply: “The esoteric meaning of emitting light!”
chapterbody 66: My reply: “Ziran (at ease, natural and free from affectation).”
chapterbody 67: I added: “Whatever task I am undertaking my mind is very focused.”
chapterbody 68: “Does the emitting of light have any esoteric meaning?”
chapterbody 69: If the Buddha’s enlightenment is the hilltop, wisdom is just a path leading to the hilltop, and so is compassion.

chapterbody: student, an analytical thinker, once said to me: “Grand Master Lu! I know now the reason behind your writing every day. It is a skillful means to guide sentient beings to the road that leads to enlightenment.”
chapterbody 3: I nodded.
chapterbody 5: He also said: “The contents of your books focus on wisdom and compassion. Although publications such as Remembrance of the Moon River, Intimate Talk with Moon, and The Dreamland Diary seem to describe emotions, they are still inherently about wisdom and compassion. Am I right in saying so?”
chapterbody 7: I again nodded.
chapterbody 9: He said finally: “I understand that wisdom and compassion are of utmost importance. I don’t think one can attain enlightenment without either. Wouldn’t you agree?”
chapterbody 11: I said: “Please listen very carefully. Wisdom and compassion are still skillful means. Both are methods to access enlightenment.”
chapterbody 13: He asked: “Is Grand Master Lu saying that wisdom is not enlightenment, and neither is compassion?”
chapterbody 15: I replied: “If the Buddha’s enlightenment is the hilltop, wisdom is just a path leading to the hilltop, and so is compassion.”
chapterbody 17: “So what is the hilltop?”
chapterbody 19: I replied: “The hilltop is the hilltop!”
chapterbody 21: “Can you reveal a little bit of the enlightenment experience?”
chapterbody 23: I gave him the following analogies:
chapterbody 25: A cloudless sunny sky.
chapterbody 26: Inside the Sun Cake there is no sun.
chapterbody 27: Inside the Moon Cake there is no moon.
chapterbody 28: Inside the Wife Cake (lao po bing) there is no wife.
chapterbody 30: A black barrel.
chapterbody 31: Moon in the water.
chapterbody 32: Flower in the mirror.
chapterbody 33: Etc.
chapterbody 35: “I don’t understand!”
chapterbody 37: I replied: “There is nothing to understand.”
chapterbody 39: “Can it be seen?”
chapterbody 41: “It cannot be seen!”
chapterbody 43: “Can it be heard?”
chapterbody 45: “It cannot be heard!”
chapterbody 47: “Can it be touched?”
chapterbody 49: “It cannot be touched!”
chapterbody 51: “Can it be felt?”
chapterbody 52: “There is nothing to be felt!”
chapterbody 53: “If one cannot see, hear, touch, or feel, then how does one know if one has attained enlightenment?”
chapterbody 54: I replied: “In the awareness that there is no seeing, hearing, touching, or knowing, one is enlightened!”
chapterbody 58: “I still don’t understand!”
chapterbody 60: Thus I said: “Did you sleep?”
chapterbody 63: “Did you have any dreams last night?”
chapterbody 65: “I did.”
chapterbody 67: “Did you wake up?”
chapterbody 68: “I did wake up.”
chapterbody 74: “When I woke up, I realized everything in the dream had been unreal.”

chapterbody: often sing this song to Ping Er, a three-year-old girl living next to the Ling Shen Ching Tze Temple in Redmond.
chapterbody 2: “When will this ailment be cured?”

chapterbody 19: By the principle of dependent origination,
chapterbody 20: Arising and disintegration are abandoned.
chapterbody 21: The Mighty One who proclaims this,
chapterbody 22: I prostrate and pay homage.
chapterbody 23: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 24: Nagarjuna Bodhisattva tells us that Shakyamuni Buddha taught the principle of dependent origination, a method to abandon existence and non-existence. To such a mighty saint, we have to prostrate in reverence!

chapterbody 28: I take refuge in the One who extinguishes the world in the three times,
chapterbody 29: By teaching the true doctrine of dependent origination;
chapterbody 30: If one understands that all dharmas arise in dependence upon other
chapterbody 31: dharmas,
chapterbody 32: One abandons desire in one’s actions and activities.
chapterbody 34: I say:
chapterbody 36: “If one recognizes all dependently arisen entities have no inherent nature; that is liberation!”
chapterbody 38: Thus another analogy:
chapterbody 40: I am not the conductor.
chapterbody 41: There is no True Buddha Train.
chapterbody 43: (This is the true nature of ultimate reality.)
chapterbody 45: I, one who comprehends the nature of mind and recognizes
chapterbody 46: Buddha-essence, have already understood the “fundamental true condition.”
chapterbody 47: Chooga, chooga, chooga.
chapterbody 48: Impermanence
chapterbody 49: Non-self
chapterbody 50: Emptiness
chapterbody 51: Suffering
chapterbody 52: True nature refers to:
chapterbody 53: All conditioned things being in a constant state of flux.
chapterbody 54: Nirvana is quiescence.
chapterbody 56: All phenomena are without a permanent self.
chapterbody 57: This is the true condition of the human world.
chapterbody 59: I once asked a medical doctor:
chapterbody 61: The doctor replied: “At the time of death, it will be gone! After death, all illnesses will be gone!”
chapterbody 62: When I heard that, I had an awakening!
chapterbody 63: After reading this, will you have an awakening?
chapterbody 65: “You get it. But, have others?”
chapterbody 69: Oh little children, hop aboard, hop aboard!
chapterbody 71: Line the little stools into a row!

chaptername: 012 Magic Plate and the Ouija Board
chapterbody: fter watching the American horror movie Ouija, a game I once played called Divination Plate (Diexian) flashed through my mind.
chapterbody 3: Divination had once been very popular.
chapterbody 5: In this game, a plate is placed on top of a piece of paper, an [Article of One Thousand Chinese Characters]. Three players would each place a hand lightly on the plate. Ask any question and the Divination plate would move on its own to give an answer.
chapterbody 7: The American Ouija board is very similar. The Ouija board is marked with numbers and letters of the alphabet. Participants place their fingers on the movable indicator which moves from letter to letter to spell out words.
chapterbody 9: At the True Buddha Quarter in the United States, I have an Ouija board which was given to me by Dharma Sister Qian as a souvenir. But I have not played it.
chapterbody 11: What I would like to ask everyone is this:
chapterbody 13: Are you pushing the indicator?
chapterbody 14: Am I pushing the indicator?
chapterbody 15: Is he pushing the indicator?
chapterbody 17: The answer is none of the above. There are indeed “spirit” media pushing the indicators.
chapterbody 19: The answers provided by the board are only sometimes right. This may be because the spirit coming to give guidance is not a high but rather low level being. Hence, the game is not straight-forward and can be quite complicated.
chapterbody 21: If one becomes too attached to the game, one may have trouble pulling away from it.
chapterbody 23: It is possible for accidents to happen to players of the Ouija and
chapterbody 24: Magic Plate, thus leading to the phenomenon of spirit possession or psychosis.
chapterbody 26: I say:
chapterbody 28: During the two hours you are in a cinema, your eyes and mind are fully invested in the movie, and your thoughts rise and fall with the story line.
chapterbody 30: When you integrate yourself into the plot of a horror movie, you become terrified and horrified.
chapterbody 32: Two hours later when the movie is over, you stand up and seem to return to reality.
chapterbody 34: When you think about the movie –
chapterbody 36: Ha!
chapterbody 38: Just a movie! You make a clean break from it and never think about it again.
chapterbody 39: That is how we watch movies most of the time. After the film, you let go!
chapterbody 41: But –
chapterbody 43: Have you thought about this? Everyone dwells on each day of their lives, yesterday, today, tomorrow… in a continuum.
chapterbody 44: Last year, this year, next year…
chapterbody 45: The past, the present, the future…
chapterbody 47: People are confused and mistake the events in this life to be in continual existence.
chapterbody 49: “Can you cut them off?”
chapterbody 51: “No.”
chapterbody 52: “Why not?”
chapterbody 53: “Because we do not think life is just a movie!”
chapterbody 54: Here let me tell everyone honestly:
chapterbody 58: “Cut everything away!”
chapterbody 60: “Live only in the moment, if there is still a moment!”
chapterbody 63: “What are you, at this moment?”
chapterbody 65: My reply: “Let it go!”
chapterbody 67: “The present – forgotten!”
chapterbody 68: “The future – not yet here!”
chapterbody 74: As long as all obstacles are removed, revealing the Buddha-essence, all living beings are one hundred percent buddhas.

chaptername: 013 I See Buddha in Every Being
chapterbody: have great admiration for the Catholic nun the Blessed Mother Teresa of India.
chapterbody 3: Her dedication and perseverance to serving the poor really touches my heart.
chapterbody 4: Shakyamuni Buddha had migraines, bone spurs, and gastrointestinal problems.
chapterbody 5: “I see God in every human being” is a famous quote of hers.
chapterbody 7: I have heard that:
chapterbody 9: She brought the sick and homeless to her congregation.
chapterbody 10: She bathed them.
chapterbody 11: Personally fed them.
chapterbody 12: Cleansed their sores with medicinal washes.
chapterbody 13: Attended to them as if they were her own parents.
chapterbody 15: In fact, Mother Teresa served all people as if they were God.
chapterbody 16: (The Vatican has beatified her, bestowing her the title “Blessed.”)
chapterbody 18: In Buddhism –
chapterbody 20: We view older women as mothers.
chapterbody 21: We view younger women as sisters.
chapterbody 22: This is to arrest “licentiousness.”
chapterbody 23: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 26: Paternal relatives on our right side.
chapterbody 27: Maternal relatives on our left side.
chapterbody 28: Behind us benefactors, teachers, and elders.
chapterbody 29: In front of us all beings in the six realms of transmigration.
chapterbody 30: (We practice Vajrayana sadhanas with all beings encircled around us so we can all attain Buddhahood together.)
chapterbody 32: Furthermore:
chapterbody 34: I see Buddha in every living being.
chapterbody 36: In this way, I am carrying the words of Shakyamuni Buddha to action.
chapterbody 38: The Buddha has said:
chapterbody 40: “All sentient beings without exception have Buddha-essence.”
chapterbody 41: Some Buddhists regard all sentient beings as their fathers and mothers from past lives”.
chapterbody 43: In Buddhism:
chapterbody 45: “We have parents in this life, and we also have parents from countless past lives. These countless rebirths owe their existence to the infinite kindness of our parents. When we recognize this, we should remember to repay their kindness and treat everyone kindly and with equanimity”.
chapterbody 47: “What kind of ideas are these of yours?”
chapterbody 48: The most important point is this:
chapterbody 49: I replied: “Immeasurable benevolence, immeasurable compassion, immeasurable joy, and immeasurable equanimity. Impartiality and Mirror-like Awareness.”
chapterbody 50: All sentient beings have Buddha-essence.
chapterbody 51: As long as all obstacles are removed, revealing the Buddhaessence, all living beings are one hundred percent buddhas.
chapterbody 52: I said to myself:
chapterbody 53: “People in the world can be so repulsive. Yet it takes all kinds of people to make this world. Some are like rotten wood that cannot be carved, some are ignoble and have no sense of shame, some are rebellious, some are victimizers, some are murderers and arsonists, some are ferocious brutes, some are criminals, some are robbers and thieves, and some are the devil incarnate. Can you really see Buddha in all these people?”
chapterbody 54: I replied: “I can!”
chapterbody 58: “What if you try to save them and end up being killed instead?”
chapterbody 60: I replied: “The Buddha had in a previous life sacrificed his own life to feed a tiger!”
chapterbody 63: I replied: “I will come again in the next life!”
chapterbody 65: I replied: “I am awakened!”
chapterbody 66: “What if they still do not show any understanding and sympathy?”
chapterbody 67: I replied: “I have no qualms about sacrificing life for the dharma!”
chapterbody 68: “What if people still ridicule you after your sacrifice?”
chapterbody 71: “What if you are victimized again in the next life?”

chaptername: 014 What to Do with the Cough?
chapterbody: returned to Taiwan on November 15, 2014. Two days later, I woke up and my voice was hoarse.
chapterbody 3: I did not mind it at first. Then I started coughing, and this turned into more and more serious and frequent coughing fits. It was such a severe, dry cough that I started heaving slightly each time.
chapterbody 5: I visited an ear, nose, and throat specialist and took medication, lozenges, and cough syrup. I finished all the medicine, but there was no improvement.
chapterbody 7: During the first week’s dharma ceremony, I would cough before going up to the dais to give a dharma discourse. During the discourse, however, I did not cough at all. After the discourse, the coughing resumed.
chapterbody 9: The same situation happened the second week. I started coughing prior ascending to the teaching dais. There was no coughing during the talk, but it resumed right afterwards.
chapterbody 11: During the Buddhist ritual “Yoga for Distributing Food to Flaming-Mouth Hungry Ghosts” at the Nectar Vihara (Ganlu Jingshe in Taichung), I did not cough at all when giving the dharma discourse. Other times, I was coughing.
chapterbody 13: It was strange! The coughing only stopped when I gave dharma discourses.
chapterbody 15: After announcing my cough in public –
chapterbody 17: Students started sending me medicine:
chapterbody 18: Cough syrups.
chapterbody 19: Herbs.
chapterbody 20: Lozenges.
chapterbody 21: Cough powders.
chapterbody 22: Effervescent tablets.
chapterbody 23: Western medicines.
chapterbody 26: With the amount of drugs accumulating in my home, I could open a pharmacy. I picked over the drugs and took some of them. To this date, as I am writing this article, I am still coughing.
chapterbody 28: I threatened the Golden Mother of Jade Pond: “If this cough does not stop, I will convert to Christianity!”
chapterbody 30: I also said to her: “If I keep coughing, I will become Muslim!”
chapterbody 32: But, the coughing continued!
chapterbody 34: Suddenly, I was confused! What to do? What to do? What to do?
chapterbody 35: What to do? What to do?
chapterbody 37: Many physician students of mine tried to help me.
chapterbody 39: I asked myself what to do?
chapterbody 41: I replied: “I don’t know!”
chapterbody 43: “Will you blame and complain indiscriminately here and there?”
chapterbody 45: I replied: “I will not!”
chapterbody 47: “Did someone put black magic or Gu poison on you?”
chapterbody 49: I replied: “Ignore it!”
chapterbody 51: “Could it be the poor air quality in Taiwan?”
chapterbody 52: I replied: “Poor air quality is a fact. But no one else in the group that came back has a cough except me. So, I cannot blame the air.”
chapterbody 53: “You use the throat to speak the dharma and this is creating such an inconvenience for you. Why not find a quick cure?”
chapterbody 54: I replied: “Let nature run its course!”
chapterbody 58: “Does a great saint, an enlightened guru, and a buddha who has realized the Buddha-essence still have to undergo physical torture?”
chapterbody 60: I replied: “Shakyamuni Buddha had migraines, bone spurs, and gastrointestinal problems.”
chapterbody 63: The powers of buddhas are unlimited, but Buddhists have limits.
chapterbody 65: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 67: I replied: “When I am well! Haha!”

chapterbody: ime: December 12, 2014, evening.
chapterbody 2: Place: The Sun Moon Lake Restaurant in Taichung.
chapterbody 3: Character: Indonesian students.
chapterbody 5: An Indonesian female student in the group paints Buddhist thangkas including those of Vajravarahi, Vajrayogini, and Vajrasattva. Her works are remarkably fine and true to life.
chapterbody 6: “When sentient beings are ill, I am ill.”
chapterbody 7: She gave a Vajravarahi thangka to Master Lianhong of Surabaya.
chapterbody 8: She gave a Vajrayogini thangka to me.
chapterbody 9: I was very happy.
chapterbody 11: Her English name is Elizabeth.
chapterbody 12: My practice has enabled me to control my desires and allowed me to transcend them freely.
chapterbody 13: She gave this account:
chapterbody 14: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 15: She was diagnosed with a three centimeter cancerous tumor in her brain. The doctors wanted to surgically excise it.
chapterbody 17: She flew to Seattle to seek blessings from me and I told her that I would give her a dream guidance.
chapterbody 19: She then had a dream where she saw Grand Master Lu wearing scrubs and using a surgical knife to operate on her.
chapterbody 21: When she returned home and went to the hospital for another examination – what a shock! The cancerous tumor was nowhere to be found.
chapterbody 23: “The doctors said it was impossible! But it had indeed disappeared!”
chapterbody 25: “The doctors said this was just too inconceivable!”
chapterbody 27: She was very happy.
chapterbody 31: “Are you a surgeon?”
chapterbody 33: “No.”
chapterbody 35: “Can you perform operations on others?”
chapterbody 39: “The surgery that you performed in this woman’s dream, what is it all about?”
chapterbody 41: “It was a dream.”
chapterbody 43: “If it were just a dream, how did the tumor disappear?”
chapterbody 45: “It was serendipity!”
chapterbody 47: “Some dreams coincide with reality while others do not. What is this all about?”
chapterbody 49: “It is still fate and affinity.”
chapterbody 51: “In dreams you become a surgeon. When those with cancerous tumors beseech you for help and you operate on them, they are healed. Wouldn’t everyone come to you for help?”
chapterbody 52: “Yes.”
chapterbody 53: “Will everyone who beseeches you be healed?”
chapterbody 54: “Not necessarily.”
chapterbody 58: “Some will get well while some will not. Isn’t this favoritism?”
chapterbody 60: I replied: “It really depends on fate and affinity!”
chapterbody 63: “The powers of buddhas are unlimited, but Buddhists have limits.”
chapterbody 65: “Aren’t you a buddha who has understood the nature of mind and recognized the Buddha-essence?”
chapterbody 67: I replied: “As an example, my hand can lift an object up to twenty kilograms. If something is within this range, I can cure it. When it weighs more than twenty kilograms, I cannot.”
chapterbody 68: “Isn’t it true that the powers of buddhas are unlimited?”

chapterbody: ianghua Lei Tsang Temple (Taichung) organized an evening party. The first program of the evening was a “belly dance.” Whoa, belly dancers!
chapterbody 2: I pictured women wearing gorgeous chiffon dresses, their navels exposed, undulating their hips and abdomens to the sound of the music.
chapterbody 6: Seven female belly dancers appeared. They were not our students but brought in from the outside. Some were plump and some were slender, and they wore colorful and dazzling costumes.
chapterbody 8: But the dance was a little different from what I had imagined:
chapterbody 10: The ladies all wore an extra piece of black tulle, covering their bellies!
chapterbody 12: I asked: “Why add the black tulle?”
chapterbody 14: Someone next to me answered: “Because Grand Master Lu is a monk.”
chapterbody 16: I said: “I am also a man.”
chapterbody 18: That person uttered “ha ha” and then hemmed and hawed!
chapterbody 20: I chatted with myself: “Should monks watch belly dancing? Look at nude paintings? Furthermore, should monks watch a striptease? Can monks…?”
chapterbody 22: “What is good?”
chapterbody 26: I said: “Most people can watch belly dancing, but, monks cannot.”
chapterbody 28: “It is very difficult to draw boundaries between good and bad.”
chapterbody 30: When others watch such a dance, it is considered a good thing.
chapterbody 31: But if monks watch, it is considered a bad thing.
chapterbody 33: Therefore, the women had to wear black tulle over their bellies, turning a belly dance into a non-belly dance!
chapterbody 35: There is a joke:
chapterbody 37: A lumberjack went into the mountains to find firewood.
chapterbody 39: Deep in the mountains he met a monk meditating.
chapterbody 41: The lumberjack asked: “Do you still have improper thoughts?”
chapterbody 43: The monk replied: “Three times a month.”
chapterbody 45: The lumberjack said: “What a profound achievement. You are indeed a skillful meditator who commands our admiration!”
chapterbody 47: The monk then said: “Three times a month, but each time for ten days.”
chapterbody 49: Ha ha ha!
chapterbody 51: I asked myself: “Would you become aroused watching belly dancing?”
chapterbody 52: I replied: “No!” (What is so nice about the belly?)
chapterbody 53: “Would you become aroused watching a striptease?”
chapterbody 54: I replied: “No.” (There is no passion or feeling involved.)
chapterbody 58: “Would you become aroused looking at a nude sketch?”
chapterbody 60: I replied: “No.” (They are just paper figures.)
chapterbody 63: I replied: “My practice has enabled me to control my desires and allowed me to transcend them freely.”
chapterbody 65: I also said: “I know I am empty, you are empty, and they are empty.”
chapterbody 67: “I don’t know about others!”
chapterbody 68: “Why are you not aroused?”
chapterbody 75: Love and hate are the same thing. Therefore, don’t be persistent and stubborn about them.

chapterbody: love the melody of “Moonlight over the Lotus Pond.” After listening to it several times, I sing a verse:
chapterbody 3: I am like a little fish in your lotus pond,
chapterbody 4: Just so I can watch over that bright white moon with you.
chapterbody 5: Swimming throughout the four seasons,
chapterbody 7: Waiting as if you are in the midst of the water.
chapterbody 9: This is a love song:
chapterbody 10: Very beautiful.
chapterbody 11: Very sweet.
chapterbody 12: But filled with “illusions.”
chapterbody 14: Because:
chapterbody 16: There will not only be one fish in the lotus pond.
chapterbody 17: The moon is not necessarily very bright and white.
chapterbody 18: Seasons change.
chapterbody 19: Lotuses in bloom wither. Will they still be fragrant?
chapterbody 20: How long will you wait?
chapterbody 21: What if you wait in vain?
chapterbody 22: I used to love the lyrics of the song “Holding Hands”:
chapterbody 23: I love your love,
chapterbody 25: So I am sad for your sadness,
chapterbody 26: And happy for your happiness.
chapterbody 27: I walk your walk,
chapterbody 28: And suffer your suffering,
chapterbody 29: So I am joyful for your joy,
chapterbody 30: And chase the same pursuits.
chapterbody 32: I am afraid to hear vows and promises,
chapterbody 33: So I trust your silence shall persuade the fate of tomorrow.
chapterbody 34: We can’t escape wind and rain or ups and downs,
chapterbody 35: So I hold your hands confidently, abandoning thoughts of turning back.
chapterbody 37: Our holding hands does not mean we had a good past life,
chapterbody 38: Our accompanying each other may make our present life even busier,
chapterbody 39: By holding hands now, we will walk together again in a future life,
chapterbody 40: On a path with company, there is no time to turn back.
chapterbody 43: It contains a mixture of sadness and happiness, as well as a sentiment of perseverance.
chapterbody 44: Finally, it mentions:
chapterbody 45: Past life.
chapterbody 46: Present life.
chapterbody 47: Future life.
chapterbody 49: Due to “causes and conditions,” “consequences” ensue, entwined with “affinities.” The lyrics are quite touching.
chapterbody 51: Next, we can ask ourselves:
chapterbody 52: “Is there love in your life?”
chapterbody 53: “Yes.”
chapterbody 54: “Do you feel alive because of this love?”
chapterbody 58: “Yes.”
chapterbody 60: “Do you have hate in your life?”
chapterbody 63: “Isn’t love the same?”
chapterbody 65: “Yes,” I replied. “Love and hate are the same thing. Therefore, don’t be persistent and stubborn about them.”
chapterbody 66: “Yes, I do. But, I quickly put it down.”
chapterbody 67: “How do you put down hate?”
chapterbody 68: I replied: “A dream, a play, it is over soon!”
chapterbody 69: “If you do not persist and are not stubborn about love, is it still love?”
chapterbody 74: I replied: “It depends on the situation!” (To truly love is to give the other person happiness.)

chapterbody: fter writing “to truly love is to give the other person happiness” in the last chapter, I feel that I have not expressed what I truly meant in my heart. So here is an essay about “giving way.”
chapterbody 3: The emotional world of humans is complex. Love is complicated and can become quite chaotic.
chapterbody 5: There are many cases of violence attributed to love:
chapterbody 7: A highly educated student fell in love with a female teacher. But, the teacher already had a lover. So the PhD student murdered the teacher by stabbing her dozens of times with a knife. With each stabbing, he cried out in tears, “I love you!”
chapterbody 10: A former husband asked his divorced wife to get back together. The two tried to negotiate. When the wife refused to forgive and forget, the ex-husband killed her.
chapterbody 11: Alas!
chapterbody 13: A young woman fell in love with her coworker. But, this coworker loved someone else. This young woman tried to seduce the coworker while the two were alone and was rebuffed. Later, this woman accused the coworker of sexual harassment.
chapterbody 14: Alas!
chapterbody 16: These “terrorist lovers” are indeed “terrifying.” When their love was unrequited, they killed the other person. If they could not have the other person, then no one else could. Is this love?
chapterbody 17: I advocate: “To love is to give the other person happiness!”
chapterbody 19: For example:
chapterbody 21: When you love someone, but that someone loves another person whose attributes are preferred to yours, give happiness to your loved one by:
chapterbody 22: “Giving way.”
chapterbody 25: The other is gold.
chapterbody 26: I will “give way.”

chapterbody: t Taiwan Lei Tsang Temple, after every fire puja ceremony, I administer “mo-ding” to followers.
chapterbody 2: I replied: “Just that I am able to care for others! We will put value aside.”
chapterbody 3: First I visualize myself becoming one with the Yidam.
chapterbody 5: Then I extend my hand and touch the crown of those present, one by one. A stream of cosmic energy flows through my hand into their bodies.
chapterbody 7: Every time during mo-ding, my heart aches:
chapterbody 8: There are those upon their last gasp.
chapterbody 9: Individuals who are intubated.
chapterbody 10: Some who are wheelchair-bound and cannot stand up.
chapterbody 11: I stroke their heads, their feet, and think of myself in their place.
chapterbody 13: How would I feel?
chapterbody 14: A blind person wants me to touch his eyes.
chapterbody 15: A deaf person wants me to touch his ears.
chapterbody 16: A mute wants me to touch his throat.
chapterbody 17: A person with an illness of the nose wants me to touch his nose.
chapterbody 18: Those with strokes, their heads.
chapterbody 19: Those with thyroid disease, their throats.
chapterbody 20: Those with lymph node diseases, their armpits.
chapterbody 21: Those with heart diseases, their hearts.
chapterbody 22: There are people with pneumonia.
chapterbody 23: Liver disease.
chapterbody 25: Kidney disease.
chapterbody 26: Stomach disease.
chapterbody 27: Intestinal disease.
chapterbody 28: Tumors both benign and malignant.
chapterbody 30: All kinds of aches and pains – swollen limbs, swollen abdomen, headaches, aches all over the body…
chapterbody 32: As I administer mo-ding, I fight back the aching in my heart and the tears welling up my eyes while chanting mantras and putting on a pleasant smile to bless each one individually.
chapterbody 34: I know: “Being sick is the worst misery!”
chapterbody 36: They hunger for my extended hand to relieve their suffering and cure their illnesses.
chapterbody 38: I do my utmost to bless them and put my feet into their shoes.
chapterbody 40: I myself also have had illnesses. I was an insomniac for years and became a bag of bones. If I bent over, I would almost faint. I felt like the walking dead.
chapterbody 42: I have also had a chronic cough so bad that my lungs vibrated and my stomach hurt. Not only was I short of breath and choking, I coughed up blood and had pneumonia.
chapterbody 44: I have experienced many illnesses and know that the agony of illness is the number one suffering in life.
chapterbody 46: Hence, the main purposes of administering mo-ding to others are:
chapterbody 48: 1. To give others happiness.
chapterbody 49: 2. To remove others’ suffering.
chapterbody 50: 3. To carry out the act with my heart and strength to the utmost.
chapterbody 51: 4. You, he, and I are equal.
chapterbody 52: I have Buddha-essence.
chapterbody 53: He has Buddha-essence.
chapterbody 56: I see all sentient beings as “buddhas.”
chapterbody 57: When I see a sick person, I have feelings born of true love and do not feel disgust.
chapterbody 59: I asked myself: “Is there any value in doing this?”
chapterbody 62: I do not have a self myself, so do I still have reputation?
chapterbody 63: Sheng-yen Lu
chapterbody 70: You have Buddha-essence.

chapterbody: n the internet, there was a period of time when some people called me:
chapterbody 2: “Scoundrel!” “Big scoundrel!” “Rotten egg!”
chapterbody 8: I have asked myself: “Are you a bad person?”
chapterbody 10: I replied: “Alas! I admit that I am not a saint nor without shortcomings. I am a seventy-year old man with quite a few shortcomings. Am I a bad person? I cannot analyze that.”
chapterbody 12: “Then are you a good person?”
chapterbody 14: I replied: “I am not good in everything. I only know how to write and meditate. I am not very good in other things. But, I do not harm others. Does this qualify me to be a good man? I am not sure.”
chapterbody 16: “Which one are you? More good and less bad, more bad and less good, equally good and bad, neither good nor bad?”
chapterbody 18: I replied: “I really cannot tell. I don’t know!”
chapterbody 20: “Right! When you were a student, did you ever cheat during an examination?”
chapterbody 22: I replied: “Yes.”
chapterbody 24: “Cheating at exams is bad, don’t you know?”
chapterbody 26: I replied: “I know. But I don’t cheat anymore. There are no more exams at seventy-years old!”
chapterbody 28: “Have you done other bad things?”
chapterbody 30: I replied: “Yes. I have stolen fruit from another person’s tree.”
chapterbody 32: “Don’t you know this is a bad thing?”
chapterbody 34: “I do.”
chapterbody 36: “How many bad things have you done altogether?”
chapterbody 38: “Too many to count.”
chapterbody 40: “Are those who criticized you good people?”
chapterbody 42: I replied: “Some of them I don’t know. Those I know are not saints either!”
chapterbody 44: “Do you regard them as enemies? You certainly are not friends!”
chapterbody 46: “I simply regard them as human beings.”
chapterbody 48: “Enemies?”
chapterbody 50: I replied: “I do not consider them enemies. In my view there are no enemies. I see Buddha-essence in every person. Every person is a
chapterbody 51: buddha. I do not make distinctions between foe and friend.”
chapterbody 52: “Do you detest those who curse you?”
chapterbody 53: I replied: “Since taking up Buddhist practice, I have become a different person. Nothing bothers me anymore. Praises are fine, curses are fine. I am indifferent to them. There is nothing to hate or not hate.”
chapterbody 54: “Don’t you want to maintain your reputation?”
chapterbody 58: I said: “I have learned the truth of suffering, emptiness, impermanence, the non-existence of an inherent self, and liberation. I do not have a self myself, so do I still have reputation?”
chapterbody 60: “If someone hits you?”
chapterbody 63: “And criticism?”
chapterbody 65: I replied: “Who cares? Scoundrel, big scoundrel, rotten egg, ha ha! That is me.”
chapterbody 67: “If someone curses you?”
chapterbody 68: “I will not curse back.”
chapterbody 74: No one can bring with him an iota when he dies!

chapterbody: r. Zhu Jinshui, a classmate of mine from the thirty-second graduating class of the undergraduate school of Surveying College, often attends the fire offerings conducted by me at Taiwan Lei Tsang Temple.
chapterbody 3: Classmate Zhu Jinshui graduated from college summa cum laude.
chapterbody 5: On December 6, 2014, Sun Fusheng, a senior schoolmate from the thirtieth graduating class, also attended my dharma ceremony. Sun Fusheng has remained in the military after graduation and reached the rank of major-general, the highest rank in the department, when he became the director of the Surveying Unit.
chapterbody 7: That day, I was especially happy.
chapterbody 9: During our school days, Sun Fusheng always wore a smile on his round face. He was a gentle person who got along well with everybody, and I found it easy to discuss things with him.
chapterbody 11: After the dharma ceremony, classmate Zhu Jinshui, senior Sun Fusheng, and I sat down together at the same table for dinner.
chapterbody 13: I said: “We are all getting old!”
chapterbody 15: Sun Fusheng said: “I am seventy-one now.”
chapterbody 17: I said: “I am seventy.”
chapterbody 19: Sun Fusheng said: “You don’t look old at all!”
chapterbody 21: I laughed: “Everyone says so. But, in reality I am a seventy-year-old man.”
chapterbody 23: I suddenly became silent. I was pondering a question. During those years in school in the Surveying College, we were young men in our twenties. Now, fifty years have flown by.
chapterbody 25: Among our classmates, Hong Jiqiong, Chen Zhenhua, Zeng Yumin, and Shen Yunhai are no longer here. I lamented when I learned of their passing.
chapterbody 27: I asked myself: “Will you die?”
chapterbody 31: “Are you afraid of losing everything? The True Buddha School, family, wife, children, grandchildren, car, and belongings?”
chapterbody 33: I replied: “No one can bring with him an iota when he dies!”
chapterbody 35: “How about your most favorite things?”
chapterbody 37: “Most favorite or most un-favorite things, they are the same!”
chapterbody 39: “How about money, sex, and fame?”
chapterbody 41: I replied: “Still the same!”
chapterbody 43: “Do you have any regrets?”
chapterbody 45: “None.”
chapterbody 47: “You have indeed seen through everything, haven’t you?”
chapterbody 49: “Yes.”
chapterbody 51: “Who has shown you how to become so detached and free?”
chapterbody 52: I replied: “Shakyamuni Buddha and Manjushri Bodhisattva.”
chapterbody 53: “What have the Buddha and the bodhisattva taught you?”
chapterbody 54: I replied: “To die is to wake up from one’s dream!”
chapterbody 58: “Are you certain you will wake up from a dream?”
chapterbody 60: I replied: “I have long awakened. I have not yet died, but I have already awakened from my dreams.”

chapterbody: n Taichung, there is a newly built Far Eastern Department Store, Top City, located next to the Shin Kong Mitsukoshi.
chapterbody 2: Sheng-yen Lu

chapterbody 7: I said: “Good!”
chapterbody 9: Taking a stroll after a meal is a very good exercise. We walked and looked around every floor before taking the escalator down to the one below.

chapterbody 13: In the watch department, there were many beautiful and fashionable watches inlaid with sparkling diamonds and crystals. But, I took a look at my own watch which was still the best, the “Sheng-yen” brand of Rolex.
chapterbody 14: So I kept on walking!
chapterbody 16: The kitchen appliances section was filled with “women’s toys.” We looked and found no use for any gadgets there.
chapterbody 18: The men’s department had no appeal since we all wore lama outfits.
chapterbody 20: We walked through the women’s department without pausing.
chapterbody 22: At the art department, elaborately designed objects made with gold, silver, and glittering jewels delighted our eyes. We stopped to enjoy them.
chapterbody 24: When we arrived at the children’s department, I thought of the modern day low fertility rate. With fewer and fewer children, how will they sell all this merchandise?
chapterbody 26: At the bedding department, I thought about the importance of bedding. We humans spend half of our lives sleeping!
chapterbody 28: Arriving at the massage chairs, alas! I wondered if I would find any free time to sit in a massage chair since my days were so busy!
chapterbody 30: At the brand name department, all the luxury designer items held no attraction to me.
chapterbody 32: Wherever I go, everyone’s eyes are already on me.
chapterbody 33: My bald head.
chapterbody 34: My lama outfit.
chapterbody 35: My dragon vest.
chapterbody 36: These are my “brand name.”
chapterbody 38: I asked myself: “Do you want to buy anything?”
chapterbody 40: I replied: “Nothing.”
chapterbody 44: I replied: “Nothing.”
chapterbody 46: “Do you want to buy a luxury home?”
chapterbody 48: I replied: “For a monk, everywhere is home.”
chapterbody 50: “At the shopping mall, there is not a thing to buy?”
chapterbody 55: “Not a thing to buy.”
chapterbody 56: I asked: “Can one buy liberation? Can one buy enlightenment?”
chapterbody 59: Many people think it strange that a monk lama writes and speaks about such feelings.
chapterbody 70: “In the future what do you want to buy?”

chaptername: 024 The Phantasmagoric Exodus: Gods and Kings
chapterbody: went to see the movie: “Exodus: Gods and Kings.”
chapterbody 3: The protagonists are:
chapterbody 5: God
chapterbody 7: Moses
chapterbody 9: Prior to this, I also watched “The Ten Commandments.” These two movies retell the same biblical story.
chapterbody 11: The Ten Commandments emphasizes the battle of wits between Moses and Pharaoh with the portrayal of a lot of stunts and miracles. I would list the newer Gods and Kings in the category of “realism,” as the miracles are suggested to be coincidental events.
chapterbody 13: Exodus describes the Hebrews’ lives as slaves in Egypt for four hundred years and how Moses later led them out of Egypt to “the Land of Canaan.”
chapterbody 15: There are two very important scenes:
chapterbody 17: 1. The killing of every Egyptian firstborn (the Passover).
chapterbody 18: 2. The crossing of the Red Sea.
chapterbody 20: In the first:
chapterbody 22: God instructed the sons of Hebrews to sprinkle the blood of lambs on the door post, and the spirit of God would pass over those homes. The firstborns of the Egyptians, including Pharaoh’s, were slain.
chapterbody 26: Moses led the Hebrews to the Red Sea, and the sea parted for them to cross. When the pursuers chased after them, the sea returned to drown out all the chariots.
chapterbody 28: The themes of the movie are:
chapterbody 30: The Egyptians reigned and enslaved the ancient Jews.
chapterbody 31: The Jews wanted to leave Egypt to found their own country.
chapterbody 32: God punished the Egyptians by bringing plagues upon them.
chapterbody 33: Finally, Egypt allowed the Jews to leave.
chapterbody 35: God’s actions are:
chapterbody 38: Callous
chapterbody 39: Ruthless
chapterbody 40: Brutal
chapterbody 41: (As said in the Dao De Jing,) “Heaven and Earth are heartless, treating creatures like a straw dog.”
chapterbody 43: (Of course, the abuse and slaughter of Hebrews committed by the Egyptians was also very brutal.)
chapterbody 45: Before God, human life is very abject. Natural disasters and slaughter are not regarded as matters of any importance.
chapterbody 47: Bright red blood filled the phantasmagoric scenes in the movie. After watching the movie, I did not feel good.
chapterbody 49: I have said before:
chapterbody 51: I respect every human being and see “buddha” in every person.
chapterbody 52: How can I possibly treat men as straw dogs?
chapterbody 53: I asked myself: “If you were God, what would you do?”
chapterbody 54: I replied: “I would be more rational!”
chapterbody 55: I do not forsake any sentient being.
chapterbody 58: “What would you have done?”
chapterbody 60: “I would have made all the Egyptians fall into slumber for a whole month, so Moses could lead the Hebrews out of Egypt.”
chapterbody 63: “It is of course appropriate.”
chapterbody 65: “Why didn’t God do that?”
chapterbody 66: “You would not kill a single soldier.”
chapterbody 67: I replied: “No killing.”
chapterbody 68: “Is this appropriate?”
chapterbody 69: I replied: “Only buddhas have no racial discrimination!”
chapterbody 75: I will only have one thought – rebirth to the Pure Land and liberation from transmigration and suffering. Namo Amituofo!

chapterbody 28: Patients were anxious.
chapterbody 30: But, the white-uniform-clad people were insensitive.

chapterbody 40: “Yes.”

chapterbody 61: “I will only have one thought – rebirth to the Pure Land and liberation from transmigration and suffering. Namo Amituofo!”
chapterbody 63: “None. The play is over!”
chapterbody 70: “If you get terminally sick and there is nothing to do except wait for death, what will you do?”
chapterbody 71: Only by dying can humans extricate themselves from sufferings of the world.

chapterbody: he veteran television actor Zhao Shun passed away at fifty-eight. I saw the news of his death on television. Zhao Shun is a True Buddha School student.
chapterbody 3: I remember when Zhao Shun suffered his first stroke many years ago. Later when he had his second stroke, he was unable to swallow his own saliva.
chapterbody 5: Someone taught him to chant the High King Avalokitesvara Sutra one thousand times. Zhao Shun took faith and started chanting. At the same time when he finished chanting the sutra one thousand times, he was able to swallow his saliva.
chapterbody 7: Was it a miracle? Was it coincidence?
chapterbody 9: In short, Zhao Shun took refuge in the True Buddha School and made much publicity about it. Later I heard that he suffered a third stroke, but he also recovered.
chapterbody 11: Recently Zhao Shun’s condition deteriorated and he was admitted into the intensive care unit in a hospital.
chapterbody 13: Zhao Shun’s wife texted Dharma Sister Xu, asking: “When will he wake up?”
chapterbody 15: I replied: “There is a fifty-fifty chance. He is at a critical moment between life and death.”
chapterbody 17: Another message asked: “In how many days will we find out?”
chapterbody 19: I replied: “In five days, there will be an answer.”
chapterbody 21: Dharma Sister Xu figured the time and coincidentally on the fifth day, Zhao Shun passed away.
chapterbody 23: Dharma Sister Xu said to me: “Please perform a bardo deliverance for Zhao Shun!”
chapterbody 25: I said: “I will.”
chapterbody 27: I asked myself: “Zhao Shun has died. How do you feel?”
chapterbody 29: I said: “Even Shakyamuni Buddha had to die. All creatures in this world will eventually die. This is a very serious matter. Only by dying can humans extricate themselves from sufferings of the world. With death, one no longer has to worry about this life.”
chapterbody 31: “So you don’t worry about death then!”
chapterbody 33: “Businessmen have to die, Buddhists have to die, politicians have to die, religious figures have to die, rich men have to die, poor men have to die, even kings and presidents have to die…”
chapterbody 35: I said: “Death is just another kind of liberation!”
chapterbody 39: Honestly, I do not take death seriously!
chapterbody 41: “Is there anything that you take seriously then?”
chapterbody 43: I replied: “Nothing!”
chapterbody 45: “How about Buddhadharma? Tantrayana Dharma? Helping sentient beings?”
chapterbody 47: I replied: “Those are the rarest and most precious things I cherish while I am alive.”
chapterbody 49: “Aren’t those something then?”
chapterbody 51: I replied: “The apex of Buddhadharma is nothingness; the apex of Tantrayana Dharma is nothingness; furthermore, the apex of helping sentient beings is nothingness. Is there anyone who can understand these words?”
chapterbody 53: However, if true wisdom is used to analyze and explore the ultimate inherent non-existence of a self, one will discover that the physical body is not the self.
chapterbody 54: Sheng-yen Lu

chapterbody 61: My soul is still awake and my self-control still exists.
chapterbody 70: I replied: “No one is Sheng-yen Lu!”

chapterbody 58: The realm of sentient beings contains neither gains nor losses, neither increases nor decreases, is neither same nor different, with neither birth nor death.
chapterbody 60: Sheng-yen Lu

chaptername: 029 One Who Knows All Esoteric Dharmas
chapterbody: omeone once asked me: “Grand Master Lu! What esoteric dharma do you know?”

chapterbody: n 1999, Miss Hanifa gave me a Bentley as a present. This luxury sedan was pearl in color, supposedly the only Bentley of this color in the United States.
chapterbody 3: Bentleys used to be a subsidiary of Rolls-Royce. In England, the elder royals drove Rolls-Royces while the younger royals drove Bentleys. Bentleys and Rolls-Royces are of the same rank.
chapterbody 5: Since I received this car, I have driven it everywhere I travel. When people see me driving the Bentley, they raise their thumbs and say, “Good car!”
chapterbody 7: I say, “Thank you!”
chapterbody 9: It is 2014 now. My Bentley is already fifteen years old. 2015 will be here soon, and this car will be sixteen. I have not changed cars.
chapterbody 11: As you know, modern cars are ever-changing. Cars’ electronic equipment and performance are constantly updating and getting better. My old Bentley, though still reputable, is not so sophisticated now. The newer Bentleys have different designs now. My old Bentley has become obsolete.
chapterbody 13: The only drawback with my car is that its battery often leaks. It needs to be recharged frequently. This is the only drawback. Everything else is still fine!
chapterbody 15: Someone said: “Grand Master Lu! You should get a new car. This one is too old!”
chapterbody 17: I said: “I am an old man! An old car matches me just right!”
chapterbody 18: That person said:
chapterbody 19: “There are so many modern luxury cars. Wouldn’t it be nice to try a different one?”
chapterbody 21: I said: “Alas! Sixteen years. I have feelings for that vehicle.”
chapterbody 23: The person said: “Do you want to possess it forever?”
chapterbody 25: I said: “Not really, I guess I am reluctant to let it go.”
chapterbody 27: That person said: “Reluctance to let something go is wanting to possess it forever!”
chapterbody 29: I said: “Is that right? I am not even sure myself.”
chapterbody 31: I chatted with myself: “Can you possess the car forever?”
chapterbody 33: I replied: “Impossible!”
chapterbody 35: “Why is it impossible?”
chapterbody 37: I replied: “The car is a composite of various auto parts which will not only age but also malfunction. The day will eventually arrive when the car breaks down beyond repair!”
chapterbody 39: I said: “I am seventy years old and will be seventy-one next year. The day will come when I will lie down and die. I also will come to an end!”
chapterbody 41: “So, there is no such thing as forever?”
chapterbody 43: I said: “No. Nothing in the world lasts forever. There are only causes and conditions that lead to gathering and dispersing!” (This is a statement of awakening.)
chapterbody 47: All these various events in life, the coming together of causes and conditions and the extinguishment of such causes and conditions, do they awaken you at all?

How does this body make an entire family sad and gloomy?

chapterbody 9: A student with the surname Feng was once on a plane that almost crashed. The plane was cruising at 20,000 to 30,000 feet altitude. Suddenly, the plane lost its power. There was darkness inside the plane. The sound of the engine completely stopped. The airplane started falling from a high altitude at a very fast speed. All one could hear was the screaming and crying of passengers inside the plane.
chapterbody 11: Student Feng thought to himself:
chapterbody 13: “I am finished!”
chapterbody 14: “I am going to die, everything is over!”
chapterbody 15: Fear and horror coursed through his body.
chapterbody 16: The plane shook turbulently.
chapterbody 17: The passengers shook violently.
chapterbody 18: Suddenly, the power came back, the engine sound resumed, lights inside the plane returned, and the airplane started ascending slowly.
chapterbody 19: Oh heavens! Everything was back to normal!
chapterbody 21: After this harrowing experience, student Feng has refused to fly. He chooses to drive no matter how long the distance. He has also stopped travelling overseas.
chapterbody 23: Student Feng said: “I have developed a fear of flying!”
chapterbody 25: He asked me: “What should I do?”
chapterbody 27: I replied: “Take a flight once a week or fly often, this is the only method!
chapterbody 29: Only this approach will help you overcome your fear of flying.”
chapterbody 31: I asked myself: “Are you afraid of flying?”
chapterbody 33: I replied: “No!”
chapterbody 35: “Why are you not afraid?”
chapterbody 37: I replied: “Every time I fly, after I am seated, I first visualize my Yidam sitting on my crown, and I chant the Yidam mantra until I can feel the Yidam’s arrival. Then I am at ease throughout the flight.”
chapterbody 39: “What happens if you encounter terrifying air turbulence?”
chapterbody 41: “I chant the Yidam mantra!”
chapterbody 43: “If the plane you are on crashes, what will you do? Are you not afraid?”

chapterbody 47: “Really?”
chapterbody 49: “Really,” I said affirmatively.
chapterbody 51: “Why are you not afraid?”
chapterbody 52: I replied: “I have turned over my divine self to my Yidam. Once the plane crashes, the Yidam will bring me to the Pure Land, so I have no fear.”

chaptername: 044 The Buddha Also Encountered Resentment and Enmity
chapterbody: hakyamuni Buddha was a great sage who had attained great awakening and enlightenment. He was also a saint who lived the quiet life of a spiritual practitioner. However, he also encountered resentment and enmity.
chapterbody 3: Here are some examples:
chapterbody 5: Six religious leaders and their groups attacked the Buddha and his teachings.
chapterbody 6: Sundari was a woman who announced that she had sexual relations with the Buddha and members of the five hundred arhats.
chapterbody 7: Cinca-manavika was a young girl who falsely claimed that she had been impregnated by the Buddha.
chapterbody 8: Devadatta was a disciple and cousin of the Buddha, also one of the eight prince monks, who later rebelled against the Buddha and departed with many of Buddha’s disciples to form their own sangha.
chapterbody 9: After the Buddha’s death, a group of opposing students announced that with the death of Shakyamuni, they would now be free.
chapterbody 10: Due to many other factors, the Buddha also developed a negative reputation that spread amongst the outside world.
chapterbody 11: Despite being such a saint, Shakyamuni Buddha also faced much criticism from the outside world.
chapterbody 13: Therefore, we can conclude that:
chapterbody 15: In this world, no matter how decent, holy, or gentlemanly you are, no matter how cautious you are in words and deeds to not offend anyone –
chapterbody 16: Still, there will be people doubting, criticizing, attacking, and destroying you.
chapterbody 18: Some of these people may be your acquaintances.
chapterbody 19: Some may be complete strangers.
chapterbody 20: Many of them, you do not know.
chapterbody 21: They also do not know you.
chapterbody 22: But, people like to gossip and spread rumors.
chapterbody 23: They criticize you.
chapterbody 25: These were the Buddha’s responses:
chapterbody 27: “Ignore them.”
chapterbody 28: “Make no objections.”

chapterbody 33: I replied: “I emulate the Buddha.”
chapterbody 35: “Do you get even a little angry?”
chapterbody 37: I replied: “It is just as well!”
chapterbody 39: “How do you resolve these annoyances in your mind?”
chapterbody 41: I replied: “It is very simple. Even when you do your best not to offend anyone and yield to your surroundings, someone will still look you up to challenge you. This happens to everyone. When I think about it this way, the annoyance disappears. Even a saint like the Buddha cannot escape it.”
chapterbody 43: “You have achieved great things through practice and meditation, why is there still such heavy gunfire against you?”
chapterbody 45: I replied: “As virtue rises, vice also rises.”
chapterbody 47: “Will you fight back?”

chapterbody 51: “When faced with such frustrations, injustice, and irrationality, how should a practitioner deal with this?”

chapterbody: fter awakening, I had a realization: “People are just playing house!”
chapterbody 3: In childhood, one studies for the sake of one’s future…
chapterbody 4: In youth, one chases after a spouse for the sake of sex, love, and fulfillment of desires.
chapterbody 5: In middle age, one devotes oneself to children, self and others, and one’s career.
chapterbody 7: To sum it up, all is establishment and accumulation of:
chapterbody 9: Wealth.
chapterbody 10: Fame.
chapterbody 11: Power.
chapterbody 12: One’s views.
chapterbody 14: This is the life of sentient beings numbering in the billions up to as many as the number of sand grains in the Ganges.
chapterbody 16: This is how people live their lives, from birth to old age. Very rarely are there individuals who can jump out of this box.
chapterbody 18: Therefore: “People are just playing house.”
chapterbody 20: Among people on earth, only a very small number take exceptional paths:
chapterbody 22: To become a buddha.
chapterbody 23: To become an immortal.
chapterbody 26: But, even if these people choose to study and become a buddha, an immortal, or a saint, those who succeed are like the claw prints left on slush by wild-geese.
chapterbody 28: If all the land on earth represents all sentient beings, the accomplished ones are like the earth imprinted with claw-prints.
chapterbody 30: After my awakening, I found that it was not easy to teach other people how to wake up. On one hand I am “playing house,” and on the other hand I know this “playing house” is completely empty, entirely fabricated, and totally non-existent.
chapterbody 32: Most people take this “playing house” to be real.
chapterbody 33: Most people cannot let go.
chapterbody 34: Most people think wealth, fame, position, attachments, spouse and children, lust, love, emotions, sexual desires, and friends etc. are real and cannot let them go.
chapterbody 36: Attachment.
chapterbody 38: Ignorance.
chapterbody 39: Constitute the “human world.”
chapterbody 40: And I, I finally realized that all are actually playing house.
chapterbody 42: As children, we are playing house.
chapterbody 43: As adults, we are playing house.
chapterbody 44: As senior citizens, we are playing house.
chapterbody 45: During happy occasions, we are playing house.
chapterbody 46: During sad occasions, we are playing house.
chapterbody 47: Even with funerals, we are playing house.
chapterbody 48: Indeed, one holds on to one’s wrong beliefs till death! In the next life, one continues to play house.
chapterbody 50: I asked myself: “This life of yours, preaching the dharma to help deliver sentient beings, is it playing house?”
chapterbody 55: I replied: “Yes.”
chapterbody 56: I replied: “Yes.”
chapterbody 57: “Spiritual cultivation, is it playing house?”
chapterbody 59: I replied: “Yes. But, I know that I am playing house. Others just don’t know it!”
chapterbody 70: “Your writing of all these books, is it playing house?”

chapterbody 10: Great Perfection. Great Perfection teachings cover many things, but they are absolutely not conflicting.
chapterbody 11: Today someone asked me, “If I sign up as a primary supplicant for today’s homa at Rainbow Temple, will my ancestors all go to the Western Pure Land?” Earlier during the homa, on her behalf, I asked the deities this question and the answer I received was, “Eighty percent.” Eighty percent is a passing score. People who have committed grave transgressions such as those in the Avici hell or those who have committed the Five Grave Offenses are excluded. If their transgressions are of the ordinary kind, these souls, despite their karmic defilements, will be reborn to the Western Pure Land. Dharma sister Chen had a fanciful notion that all her ancestors would be able to be delivered today.

chapterbody 15: We have an especially large number of primary supplicants today, more than usual. We all have similar wishes. We hope that by becoming a primary supplicant, our ancestors will all be delivered at once so that we won’t have to sign them up for deliverance again. This is how it is. When you engender a desire to help your ancestors, the buddhas and bodhisattvas will receive your message.

I will bring forward the Innermost Essence of Nyingthig so that everyone will say: “Oh! So this is what true Buddhadharma is!” (audience applause)
chapterbody 17: The power of Buddhadharma is boundless, and the buddhas and bodhisattvas will employ many powers in their task of guiding beings in the nether world. But not everyone is able to accept that guidance. There are some who do not wish to go to the Western Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss, and instead may wish to go to the East Pure Land, South Pure Land, North Pure Land, or even stay put in samsara. For such beings, there is nothing we can do. Ultimately, if someone has affinity with the Western Pure Land, he will receive guidance and be accepted there.
chapterbody 18: What is Nyingthig? I will give you an analogy. A king asked two artisans to create a mouse sculpture. The first sculpture was extremely lifelike whereas the second one did not look much like a mouse. When the king announced that the first artisan had won, the second artisan suggested that a cat be the judge. When a cat was brought out, he liked the second instead of the lifelike one. Why? Because the second artisan had used fish bones as raw material for his sculpture. This is how it is when we discuss Nyingthig. We must seize the core if we want to get at the Innermost Essence of Nyingthig. Although un-lifelike, it may turn out to be really good stuff. On the other hand, an extremely lifelike model, due to its different raw materials, may not turn out to be a good thing. Everyone now knows that Nyingthig is innermost essence. It is something you want and need.

Which would you prefer?” The passenger then asked, “Do you have Coca Cola?” “Yes, we do. Would you like ice with that?” The passenger finally said, “Give me a cup of coffee!”
chapterbody 20: A teacher asked a student, “To use a metaphor, if you are beautiful flowers, then who am I?” The student replied, “I do not know.” The teacher said, “Think again. Who is the one in the garden with a pair of scissors cutting the flowers?” The student replied, “A flower thief!” Actually the answer is “a gardener.” The teacher is a gardener planting and caring for his garden.

chapterbody 23: Grand Master is the teacher who will teach you the nine yanas of Great Perfection, and I must first adjust your views so you understand the importance of its lineages, and its origin.
chapterbody 24: Only Amituo Tathagata’s Western Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss is offered today. Those who do not have affinity cannot go there. Those who have committed the Five Grave Offenses or transgressions sending them to the Avici hell cannot go there because their karmic obscuration is too heavy. They have no affinity with the Buddhadharma. There are some who actually oppose the Buddhadharma, and of course there is no way they will go there.
chapterbody 25: Nyingma is the oldest among all Tibetan schools. It was brought to Tibet from India by Padmasambhava.
chapterbody 26: By reciting Amitabha’s name, we achieve two important merits. One is to accumulate fortune and capital for rebirth to the Pure Land. The other is to train our mind to achieve one-pointedness. When the mind is at one-pointedness, our wishes will materialize. Buddha has said that when one is able to focus the mind, there is nothing that cannot be accomplished. If one’s mind is focused and without deviation throughout his spiritual cultivation, he will go to the Pure Land. If the mind is in turmoil at the time of death, or a chattering and unfocused mind which is suddenly on one complicated issue and the next minute on another unresolved emotional knot, one will not be able to sever mundane karma in order to be reborn to the Pure Land. Hence, we must chant “Namo Amituofo,” then chant the names of his attendants: “Namo Guanshiyin Pusa, Namo Dashizhi Pusa.” Chant each name sincerely, and bow sincerely with each chanting, with a focused mind. This is the key to recitation of a buddha’s epithet.
chapterbody 27: At that time, Tibet’s religion was Bonpo instead of Buddhist Tantrayana. Tantrayana only started in Tibet after Padmasambhava introduced it. The teachings that Padmasambhava transmitted are the Great Perfection Nine Yanas. Padmasambhava is an emanation of the unification of the three deities Amitabha, Shakyamuni Buddha, and Avalokitesvara.
chapterbody 28: In the future I will write about the “Pathway of Pure Land” and teach everyone a method to chant a buddha’s epithet. After this method is published, you can refer to it in your practice. Even elderly practitioners can do this. When one is young, one should hear and learn more practices! In middle age, one should concentrate to penetrate one specific practice deeply. In old age, one should pray for rebirth to the Buddha-land. This is a most important point which was also taught by earlier Buddhist practitioners – to remain one-pointed in seeking rebirth to the Pure Land.
chapterbody 29: Padmasambhava is also a kumara (youth) manifested from a lotus, born out of a lotus without a father or a mother, found lying in a lotus. Padmasambhava is also a Padmakumara. They are one and the same. Hence, Padmasambhava is one of us Padmakumaras. (audience applause) The roots are very deep! Grand Master is Padmakumara. Padmasambhava is also Padmakumara, as he was originally manifested and born from a lotus!
